input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin (DT) system** primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (as per [7]) and **automotive manufacturing systems** (as per [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [17]). The **physical twin** is characterized as a **complex, interconnected system** with the following key attributes:

1. **Type and Domain**:
   - **Automotive Industry**: The system under study includes **vehicle manufacturing processes**, **battery systems** ([17]), and **entire automotive supply chains** ([8], [9]). It spans **design, production, and operational phases** of vehicles, including **electrical/electronic (E/E) architectures**, **battery management systems (BMS)**, and **on-board diagnostics**.
   - **Cyber-Biophysical Systems**: These systems integrate **physical components** (e.g., sensors, actuators, mechanical parts) with **cyber components** (e.g., control algorithms, IoT devices, cloud-based analytics) to enable **real-time monitoring and adaptive control** ([7]).
   - **Aerospace Applications**: Extended to **space-based systems** (e.g., **on-orbit collision avoidance** and **space debris detection**) ([16]), where the physical twin involves **satellite subsystems**, **orbital mechanics models**, and **sensor networks**.

2. **Main Components**:
   - **Physical Layer**: Includes **sensors (e.g., IoT/IIoT sensors for temperature, vibration, pressure)**, **actuators**, **mechatronic systems**, and **production machinery** (e.g., CNC machines, assembly lines).
   - **Cyber Layer**: Comprises **embedded systems**, **edge/fog computing nodes** ([14]), **cloud platforms**, and **AI/ML models** for predictive analytics.
   - **Information Layer**: Encompasses **digital models** (e.g., **CAD/CAM models**, **finite element analysis (FEA) models**), **simulation engines**, and **data repositories** (e.g., **historical production data**, **real-time telemetry**).
   - **Human Layer**: Involves **operators**, **engineers**, **maintenance technicians**, and **decision-makers** who interact with the DT for **diagnostics, optimization, and training**.

3. **Operational Domain**:
   - **Manufacturing**: **Real-time monitoring of production lines**, **quality control**, and **predictive maintenance** ([10], [12], [14]).
   - **Automotive**: **Vehicle development lifecycle** (from concept to end-of-life), **battery health monitoring**, and **fleet management** ([8], [9], [17]).
   - **Cyber-Biophysical**: **Adaptive control** of hybrid systems (e.g., **human-machine interfaces**, **robotics**), **environmental interaction modeling**, and **resilience testing** ([7]).
   - **Aerospace**: **Orbital dynamics simulation**, **debris collision prediction**, and **system health monitoring** ([16]).

4. **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
   - **Dynamic and Evolving**: The system undergoes **continuous updates** via **real-time data streams** from sensors and **AI-driven model refinement** ([7], [15]).
   - **Multi-Scale**: Models span **microscopic (e.g., battery cell-level)** to **macroscopic (e.g., supply chain-level)** scales ([9], [17]).
   - **Interoperability**: Supports **heterogeneous data formats** (e.g., **STEP, XML, JSON**) and **standardized communication protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA, MQTT, DDS**) ([12], [14]).
   - **Federated Architecture**: Enables **distributed DTs** (e.g., **edge DTs** for local processing, **cloud DTs** for global analytics) ([14]).
   - **Digital Thread Integration**: Links **design, manufacturing, and operational data** via **digital threads** ([10], [12]).
   - **Resilience and Adaptability**: Designed for **failure mode analysis**, **adaptive control strategies**, and **post-disaster recovery** ([7]).
   - **Energy-Aware**: Optimizes **power consumption** in **edge/fog computing nodes** and **battery systems** ([14], [17]).
   - **Regulatory Compliance**: Aligns with **ISO 23247** ([10], [14], [16]) and **ISO/IEC 30173** ([11]) for **terminology, architecture, and interoperability standards**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) system** provides a **comprehensive suite of services** tailored to **automotive, manufacturing, cyber-biophysical, and aerospace domains**, categorized by **functional types**, **target users**, and **capabilities**. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services**:
   - **Real-Time Telemetry**: Continuous **sensor data acquisition** (e.g., **vibration, temperature, pressure**) from **physical assets** ([7], [8], [14]).
   - **Condition Monitoring**: **Predictive maintenance** via **machine learning-based anomaly detection** in **battery systems** ([17]) and **manufacturing equipment** ([10]).
   - **Health Monitoring**: **Space-based DTs** track **satellite subsystem health** and **orbital debris proximity** ([16]).
   - **Operational Analytics**: **Dashboards** for **production line efficiency**, **energy consumption**, and **resource utilization** ([14]).

2. **Optimization Services**:
   - **Process Optimization**: **AI-driven optimization** of **manufacturing workflows**, **supply chain logistics**, and **vehicle assembly sequences** ([9], [12]).
   - **Energy Optimization**: **Edge computing-based DTs** optimize **power distribution** in **battery systems** and **industrial IoT devices** ([14], [17]).
   - **Resource Allocation**: **Dynamic scheduling** of **production resources** and **maintenance teams** ([10]).
   - **Trajectory Optimization**: For **aerospace DTs**, **real-time trajectory adjustments** to avoid **collisions with space debris** ([16]).

3. **Prediction Services**:
   - **Failure Prediction**: **Time-to-failure (TTF) forecasting** for **mechanical components**, **electrical systems**, and **battery cells** using **physics-informed ML models** ([7], [17]).
   - **Performance Prediction**: **Lifetime estimation** of **automotive parts** and **manufacturing tools** ([9]).
   - **Environmental Impact Prediction**: **Carbon footprint modeling** for **production processes** ([12]).
   - **Cyber-Physical Resilience**: **Adaptive control strategies** to mitigate **disruptions** in **hybrid systems** ([7]).

4. **Control Services**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: **Real-time actuation** of **robotic systems**, **assembly lines**, and **vehicle dynamics** via **DT-driven feedback loops** ([8], [14]).
   - **Autonomous Decision-Making**: **AI agents** execute **autonomous corrective actions** (e.g., **re-routing production**, **adjusting battery charging**) ([15]).
   - **Human-Machine Collaboration**: **Augmented reality (AR) interfaces** for **operators** to interact with **DT simulations** ([7]).

5. **Visualization Services**:
   - **3D/4D Visualization**: **Immersive DT environments** (e.g., **VR/AR**) for **design review**, **training**, and **troubleshooting** ([7], [15]).
   - **Digital Mock-Up (DMU)**: **Collaborative 3D models** for **stakeholder alignment** in **automotive and aerospace** ([10], [16]).
   - **Data Exploration**: **Interactive dashboards** with **time-series analytics**, **geospatial mapping**, and **multi-modal data fusion** ([14]).

6. **Simulation Services**:
   - **Digital Prototyping**: **High-fidelity simulations** of **vehicle dynamics**, **battery degradation**, and **manufacturing processes** ([9], [17]).
   - **What-If Analysis**: **Scenario testing** for **failure modes**, **supply chain disruptions**, and **regulatory compliance** ([10]).
   - **Physics-Based Modeling**: **Coupled multi-physics simulations** (e.g., **thermal-mechanical-electrical**) for **cyber-biophysical systems** ([7]).

7. **Target Users and Capabilities**:
   - **Engineers**: **Design validation**, **performance tuning**, and **failure analysis** ([10], [15]).
   - **Manufacturers**: **Quality control**, **yield optimization**, and **predictive maintenance** ([12], [14]).
   - **Operators**: **Real-time monitoring**, **AR-assisted troubleshooting**, and **adaptive control** ([7], [8]).
   - **Decision-Makers**: **Strategic planning**, **risk assessment**, and **resource allocation** ([9], [16]).
   - **Maintenance Teams**: **Proactive maintenance scheduling** and **root-cause analysis** ([17]).
   - **Regulators**: **Compliance verification** and **safety certification** via **DT-driven validation** ([10], [11]).

8. **Service Enablers**:
   - **AI/ML Integration**: **Reinforcement learning (RL)**, **deep learning (DL)**, and **physics-based models** for **predictive and adaptive services** ([7], [15]).
   - **Edge Computing**: **Low-latency processing** for **real-time control** in **automotive and aerospace DTs** ([14], [16]).
   - **Cloud-Edge Synergy**: **Hybrid architectures** for **scalable analytics** and **local decision-making** ([14]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT) system** leverages a **diverse set of tools, frameworks, and enablers** to enable **real-time interoperability, AI-driven analytics, and scalable deployment**. Key components include:

1. **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
   - **ISO 23247-Compliant Frameworks**:
     - **Digital Twin Framework (ISO 23247-1:2021)** as the **standardized architectural blueprint** for **manufacturing and automotive DTs** ([10], [14], [16]).
     - **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** for **terminology and conceptual modeling** ([11]).
   - **Cloud Platforms**:
     - **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**, **AWS IoT TwinMaker**, and **Google Cloud Digital Twin** for **scalable DT deployment** ([12], [14]).
     - **Edge-Fog Computing Frameworks**: **Kubernetes-based edge clusters** and **Fog Computing platforms** (e.g., **OpenFog Consortium**) for **low-latency processing** ([14]).
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **MATLAB/Simulink** for **control system design** and **real-time simulation** ([7], [15]).
     - **ANSYS** and **COMSOL Multiphysics** for **multi-domain simulations** (e.g., **thermal, structural, fluid dynamics**) ([9], [17]).
     - **Gazebo** and **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for **robotics and autonomous systems** ([7]).
     - **Battery Simulation Tools**: **PSAT (PSAT Battery Model)** and **Dymola** for **electrochemical modeling** ([17]).
   - **AI/ML Tools**:
     - **TensorFlow**, **PyTorch**, and **scikit-learn** for **predictive analytics** and **anomaly detection** ([7], [15]).
     - **Reinforcement Learning (RL) Libraries**: **Stable Baselines3**, **RLlib** for **adaptive control strategies** ([15]).
     - **Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)** for **data-scarce cyber-biophysical systems** ([7]).

2. **Data Management and Databases**:
   - **Digital Thread Platforms**:
     - **Siemens Teamcenter**, **PTC Windchill**, and **SAP Digital Twin** for **end-to-end data integration** ([10], [12]).
   - **Time-Series Databases**:
     - **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**, and **Apache Cassandra** for **real-time sensor data storage** ([14]).
   - **Graph Databases**:
     - **Neo4j** for **relationship modeling** in **supply chain and cyber-physical networks** ([12]).
   - **Blockchain for DTs**:
     - **Hyperledger Fabric** and **Ethereum** for **secure, decentralized DT data sharing** ([12]).

3. **Communication Protocols and Standards**:
   - **Industrial IoT Protocols**:
     - **OPC UA** for **machine-to-machine (M2M) communication** ([10], [12]).
     - **MQTT/SN** for **lightweight IoT messaging** in **edge DTs** ([14]).
     - **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** for **real-time data dissemination** ([12]).
   - **5G/6G Enablers**:
     - **Ultra-Low Latency (ULL) networks** for **aerospace and automotive DTs** ([16]).
     - **Network Slicing** to prioritize **critical DT traffic** ([14]).

4. **Development Tools and IDEs**:
   - **Model-Based Design Tools**:
     - **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for **requirements modeling** ([10]).
     - **Capella** (Thales Group) for **systems engineering** ([7]).
   - **Version Control and Collaboration**:
     - **GitLab**, **Jira**, and **Confluence** for **DT development lifecycle management** ([12]).
   - **Low-Code/No-Code Platforms**:
     - **Microsoft Power Platform**, **AppSheet** for **rapid DT dashboard development** ([14]).

5. **Specialized Technologies**:
   - **Digital Twin as a Service (DTaaS)**:
     - **SaaS-based DT solutions** (e.g., **Siemens MindSphere**, **PTC ThingWorx**) for **plug-and-play DT deployment** ([12]).
   - **Quantum Computing**:
     - **Hybrid quantum-classical algorithms** for **optimization in large-scale DTs** ([15]).
   - **Digital Twin of Things (DTot)**:
     - **Micro-DTs** for **individual components** (e.g., **battery cells**, **sensors**) within a **federated DT architecture** ([13]).
   - **AR/VR for DT Interaction**:
     - **Unity**, **Unreal Engine**, and **Microsoft HoloLens** for **immersive DT visualization** ([7], [15]).
   - **Edge AI Accelerators**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson**, **Intel OpenVINO**, and **Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride** for **on-device AI inference** ([14]).

6. **Security and Resilience Enablers**:
   - **Zero-Trust Architecture**: **Identity-aware proxy (IAP)** and **continuous authentication** for **DT security** ([12]).
   - **Federated Learning**: **Privacy-preserving AI training** across **distributed DTs** ([15]).
   - **Redundancy and Failover**: **Multi-cloud DT deployment** with **automatic failover mechanisms** ([14]).

7. **Standards and Compliance**:
   - **ISO 23247** for **DT architecture and interoperability** ([10], [14], [16]).
   - **IEC 62368-1** for **electrical safety in DT systems** ([12]).
   - **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** for **DT security** ([12]).
   - **Aerospace Standards**: **ECSS-E-ST-50-12C** for **space DTs** ([16]).

8. **Deployment Models**:
   - **On-Premise DTs**: For **high-security industries** (e.g., **defense, aerospace**) ([16]).
   - **Hybrid Cloud-Edge DTs**: **Edge nodes** for **local processing**, **cloud** for **global analytics** ([14]).
   - **Federated DTs**: **Interconnected DTs** across **supply chain partners** ([13]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application standards despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by the ROS2-based architecture, where each Function Entity (FE) executes as an independent ROS2 package with nodes handling real-time data processing (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). Latency requirements for data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication are implicitly managed by the *Physical Twin Entity*, which interfaces directly with sensors/actuators. No explicit mention of service-level time scales (e.g., per-FE latency targets) is provided beyond the 100 ms synchronization constraint.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary *Function Entities* (FEs) mapped to distinct hardware components: *User Entity* (hosting *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents), *Digital Twin Entity* (managing data/control logic), and *Physical Twin Entity* (handling sensors/actuators and general automotive functionality like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). This structure deviates from ISO 23247 by consolidating resource-specific and general functionality under the *Physical Twin Entity*, which may impact ownership and engineering processes. No explicit support for **multiple Digital Twin instances** or hierarchical coordination between twins is documented; instead, the design emphasizes **single-instance deployment** with clear scope separation via entity boundaries. Coordination between entities relies on ROS2 inter-node communication, though no explicit mechanisms for cross-twin synchronization are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **ROS2-based microservices architecture**, where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an independent ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. This enables modular orchestration, with the *User Entity* (UI components), *Digital Twin Entity* (data/control logic), and *Physical Twin Entity* (resource-specific operations) deployed on separate hardware. Resource management is implicit, leveraging the entity-based structure to partition responsibilities: the *Physical Twin Entity* hosts both resource-specific (e.g., sensors/actuators) and general automotive functions (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). System-wide coordination is achieved via ROS2’s publish-subscribe model, though no explicit mention of higher-level orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, edge orchestration) or cross-entity conflict resolution mechanisms is provided. The design aligns loosely with ISO 23247’s Reference Architecture (RA) but introduces deviations (e.g., expanded *Physical Twin Entity* scope) to accommodate vehicular-specific requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** standard, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where computational models and data are structured into distinct **Functional Entities (FEs)**. The system comprises **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, which include physical entities such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, and processes. The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** digitally represents OMEs using **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** to simulate physical attributes and operational behaviors.

Data sources are managed via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with OMEs to acquire real-time and historical data. The standard does not specify exact model types beyond their functional roles (e.g., simulation, monitoring), but **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is referenced as a potential implementation for interoperability between entities. Model relationships are defined by the **entity-based RA**, where each FE (e.g., data transformation, user interface) interacts with others to maintain consistency between physical and digital representations. Data management approaches are implied to be **entity-specific**, with no explicit mention of storage formats (e.g., databases, ontologies) or synchronization protocols beyond the RA’s functional decomposition.

The **Digital Twin Entity** acts as the core computational model, while **Functional Entities** handle specialized tasks like data translation, user interaction, or OME control. The standard does not detail specific model fidelity metrics or data granularity but emphasizes **modularity** and **interoperability** as foundational principles for integrating diverse data sources and models.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **ISO 23247** standard implicitly addresses fidelity and validity through its **entity-based reference architecture**, which mandates that **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** must accurately represent **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**. While the standard does not prescribe explicit validation methods, it implies that **uncertainty quantification** and **error detection** are inherent to the system’s design, as each entity (e.g., Data Collection, Digital Twin, User) must ensure consistency between physical and digital representations.

The standard does not specify **formal verification processes**, **uncertainty sources**, or **quality assurance protocols** beyond the requirement for **functional alignment** between entities. However, the **modularity** of Functional Entities (FEs) suggests that validation could be achieved through **interoperability testing** and **data consistency checks** at entity boundaries. No quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy thresholds, sampling frequencies) or correction mechanisms (e.g., machine learning-based error mitigation) are detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors and their transformation into digital representations. **Data flows** originate from **LiDAR**, **motor actuation**, **steering servo**, and **vehicle state sensors**, which generate raw inputs including:
- **Point Cloud Data** (raw LiDAR scans, unstructured 2D/3D point representations),
- **Vehicle State Information** (e.g., speed, acceleration, orientation),
- **Actuation Signals** (motor/servo commands and feedback).

**Preprocessing Steps** are executed by the **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)**:
1. **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**: Converts raw LiDAR point clouds into structured object tracks and environmental maps using clustering algorithms (e.g., DBSCAN or similar).
2. **Data Validation**: Ensures data integrity via **unit conversions** and **serialization** (handled by the **Data Translation FE**).
3. **Real-Time Processing**: Occurs at **sensor acquisition frequency** (not explicitly stated, but implied to be high-frequency for autonomous driving, e.g., 10–100Hz for LiDAR).

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- **ROS2 Network**: Intra-system communication between SW components (e.g., Data Pre-Processing FE → Visualization FE).
- **Wi-Fi (UDP)**: Inter-entity communication for transmitting processed data (e.g., object tracks, vehicle state) to the **Digital Twin Entity** for visualization or control.
- **Event Triggers**: Dynamic updates triggered by sensor events (e.g., LiDAR scans, state changes) or time-based polling.

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- **Unit Conversions**: Standardized via the **Data Translation FE** to ensure consistency across heterogeneous data streams.
- **Message Alignment**: Serialization of data into standardized message formats (e.g., ROS2 topics or custom UDP payloads) for downstream processing.
- **No explicit mention of redundancy checks, error handling, or anomaly detection** in the provided documents.

**Outputs**:
- Processed data (e.g., object tracks, driving path projections) are fed into the **Visualization FE** for real-time 3D rendering (as shown in Fig. 3a) and **Reporting FE** for storage/generation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS facilitates closed-loop control by translating digital commands into physical actuation. **Control flows** originate from the **Controlling FE**, which interfaces with the **Actuation FE** to execute commands on the physical system. Key components include:

**Command Types**:
- **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) Commands**: Adjust vehicle speed and acceleration based on digital twin projections (e.g., object tracks, path planning).
- **Actuation Signals**: Direct motor/steering servo commands derived from control algorithms (e.g., PID or model predictive control).

**Control Message Formats**:
- **Data Translation FE**: Serializes commands into standardized formats (e.g., ROS2 topics or UDP payloads) for inter-entity communication.
- **Message Structure**: Likely includes:
  - **Header**: Timestamp, source ID, and message type (e.g., `ACC_Speed_Adjust`).
  - **Payload**: Numeric values (e.g., target speed in km/h, steering angle in degrees) and metadata (e.g., confidence scores for path projections).

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
- **No explicit mention of authentication** (e.g., cryptographic signatures, TLS) in the documents.
- **Data Validation**: Implicit via **unit conversions** and **message alignment** in the Data Translation FE to ensure commands are physically feasible (e.g., servo angle limits).

**Feedback Systems**:
- **Closed-Loop Control**: The **Actuation FE** receives feedback from physical sensors (e.g., motor/servo encoders, vehicle state sensors) and feeds it back into the **Controlling FE** for real-time adjustments.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Not explicitly detailed, but likely handled via **priority-based scheduling** or **time-sensitive networking (TSN)** mechanisms (implied by real-time requirements).

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- **ACK/NACK Mechanisms**: Not described, but assumed to exist for critical commands (e.g., emergency braking) via **UDP acknowledgments** or ROS2 feedback topics.
- **Execution Logging**: Commands are likely logged in the **Reporting FE** for post-processing analysis.

**Security Protocols**:
- **No explicit mention of encryption** or **access control** for control signals in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin System’s Technical Connection** relies on a **multi-layered network architecture** designed for real-time, low-latency communication. Key details include:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **Intra-System (ROS2)**: Software components (e.g., Data Pre-Processing FE, Controlling FE) communicate via **ROS2**, a middleware framework optimized for robotics and real-time systems. Topics and services are used for message passing.
- **Inter-Entity (Wi-Fi)**: Physical-to-Digital and Digital-to-Physical communication occurs over **Wi-Fi (UDP)** at the transport layer, enabling lightweight, broadcast-capable data exchange between entities (e.g., vehicle sensors ↔ Digital Twin Entity).

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Star Topology**: Centralized Digital Twin Entity acts as a hub, receiving data from peripheral Physical Twin Entities (e.g., vehicle sensors) and distributing commands.
- **Hierarchical Layers**:
  1. **Sensor Layer**: Raw data acquisition (LiDAR, motors, steering).
  2. **Processing Layer**: ROS2-based SW components (Data Pre-Processing, Controlling, Visualization).
  3. **Application Layer**: User interfaces (3D Visualization, Control Panel) and reporting systems.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **Not explicitly quantified**, but implied to be stringent for autonomous driving:
  - **LiDAR Data**: ~10–100Hz with point cloud sizes in the order of **MB per scan** (depending on resolution).
  - **Control Signals**: Sub-10ms latency for critical commands (e.g., emergency braking).
- **UDP’s lack of reliability** is mitigated by **application-layer retransmissions** or **ROS2 QoS policies** (e.g., history depth, reliability settings).

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **No explicit redundancy** (e.g., failover nodes, dual Wi-Fi links) is mentioned in the documents.
- **ROS2 QoS Policies**: Likely configured for **best-effort delivery** with configurable history buffers to handle occasional packet loss.
- **Data Translation FE**: Acts as a **single point of failure** for serialization/deserialization; no redundancy is implied.

**Security Protocols**:
- **Transport Layer**: UDP (unencrypted) for inter-entity communication.
- **No mention of**:
  - **Encryption** (e.g., TLS for Wi-Fi links).
  - **Authentication** (e.g., mutual TLS, digital signatures).
  - **Firewall/Network Segmentation** to isolate critical control channels.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin System’s Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is **edge-centric**, prioritizing low-latency processing for real-time applications. Key details include:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **Edge Deployment**: Primary processing occurs on **on-premise/edge devices** (e.g., vehicle onboard computers or dedicated Digital Twin servers) to minimize cloud latency.
- **No explicit mention of cloud hosting**, but **Data Storage** and **Reporting FE** could leverage cloud services for archival or analytics (not detailed in documents).

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **Computational**:
  - **Data Pre-Processing FE**: Requires **GPU acceleration** for point cloud clustering/tracking (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson or similar).
  - **Controlling FE**: Real-time control algorithms (e.g., ACC) demand **multi-core CPUs** with low-latency I/O (e.g., Intel Xeon or ARM Cortex-A).
  - **Visualization FE**: **GPU-accelerated rendering** for 3D environments (e.g., Unity/Unreal Engine integration).
- **Storage**:
  - **Raw Data**: LiDAR point clouds and sensor logs stored temporarily on **edge storage** (e.g., NVMe SSDs).
  - **Processed Data**: Object tracks, reports, and visualizations stored in **structured databases** (e.g., PostgreSQL or custom NoSQL) via the **Reporting FE**.

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Horizontal Scaling**: Not explicitly supported; system appears **monolithic** with centralized Digital Twin Entity.
- **Modularity**: SW components (e.g., Data Pre-Processing, Controlling) are **ROS2-based**, allowing potential **microservices deployment** if scaled horizontally.
- **No mention of containerization** (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) or **serverless** architectures.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **On-Premise/Edge**: Primary model for real-time processing (e.g., vehicle-mounted systems).
- **Hybrid**: Possible **cloud-edge synergy** for non-critical data (e.g., historical reports), but not detailed.

**Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
- **High Availability**: Implied via **real-time processing** and **closed-loop control**, but no redundancy mechanisms (e.g., failover, load balancing) are described.
- **Fault Tolerance**:
  - **No explicit recovery mechanisms** for component failures (e.g., crashed Controlling FE).
  - **Data Translation FE** is critical; its failure would halt all inter-entity communication.
- **Redundancy**: None mentioned for sensors, network links, or processing units.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The ISO 23247 standard outlines a **reference architecture (RA)** for Digital Twins (DTs) in manufacturing, structured around **entities** that collectively define the DT’s functionality. While the standard does not explicitly detail the **development stages** or **version control**, it provides a **modular framework** where:

    - **Entities** (e.g., Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) are responsible for distinct functionalities.
    - **Functional Entities (FEs)** are smaller units within each entity, handling specific tasks like data transformation, user interaction, or OME control. The standard does not prescribe implementation details (e.g., OPC UA is mentioned as a possible choice for interfacing).
    - **Transition management** between phases is implied through the **entity-based RA**, where each entity’s role is clearly defined, but lifecycle governance (e.g., deployment, updates, or retirement) is not explicitly described.
    - **Quality assurance** and **testing/validation** are not detailed, though the modular design suggests modular testing of FEs and entities. Version control or evolution management strategies are not addressed in the provided text.

    The standard focuses on **architectural organization** rather than procedural workflows for development or evolution.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The document does not provide explicit details on **decision support capabilities**, **analytics**, or **user interaction methods** for the Digital Twin. However, the **User Entity** is described as providing interfaces for users to interact with the Digital Twin Entity, which could imply:
    - **Monitoring data** (e.g., real-time or historical) for decision-making.
    - **Control interfaces** for OMEs (e.g., adjusting manufacturing processes).
    - **Human-interpretable data transformation** (via FEs) to support insights.

    No specifics are given on **confidence measures**, **predictive analytics**, or **automated recommendations**. The focus is on **structural roles** rather than operational decision-making workflows.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The document explicitly references the **ISO 23247:2021 standard** for Digital Twins in manufacturing, which includes:
    - **Four parts**: Overview, Reference Architecture (RA), Digital Representation of Physical Elements, and Information Exchange.
    - **Entity-based RA**: Defines roles for OMEs, Data Collection/Device Control Entities, Digital Twin Entities, and User Entities, with **Functional Entities (FEs)** as modular components.
    - **Interoperability**: Implies adherence to standards like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interfacing (though not mandatory).
    - **Compliance frameworks**: The standard itself serves as a framework for DT development, but no additional certification requirements or industry-specific standards (e.g., IEC, IEEE) are mentioned.
    - **Information exchange**: Part 4 of the standard likely addresses data formats or protocols, though specifics are not detailed in the provided text.

    The document does not mention **certification requirements** or broader **interoperability standards** beyond OPC UA as an example.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents incorporates **ISO 26262-1:2018** functional safety standards for **road vehicles**, mandating a structured approach to **cybersecurity and safety-critical system considerations**. Key security measures include:
- **Cybersecurity protocols** aligned with **ISO 26262-1:2018**, emphasizing **risk assessment** through **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** decomposition to mitigate threats in **safety-critical systems** (e.g., adaptive cruise control systems referenced in **ISO 15622:2018**).
- **Access control mechanisms** and **authentication systems** are implied for **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services** as per **3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0**, though explicit details on granular access policies or cryptographic standards (e.g., TLS, ECC) are not provided.
- **Fail-safe mechanisms** are mandated for **safety-critical functions**, requiring **redundancy, fault tolerance, and deterministic response times** to ensure system integrity under **cyber-physical attacks** or hardware failures.
- **Threat mitigation strategies** are inferred from **ISO 26262-1:2018**, which mandates **systematic risk analysis** (e.g., **FMEA, FTA**) to identify vulnerabilities in **digital twin components** (e.g., real-time simulation models, sensor data feeds).
- **Safety-critical system considerations** are explicitly tied to **automotive safety standards**, where **digital twin cockpits** (as per **Dalibor et al., 2020**) must adhere to **ISO 26262-1:2018** for **safety validation**, including **hardware/software partitioning** and **safety mechanisms** for **interactive simulations**.
- **No explicit mention of data ownership, privacy protection (e.g., GDPR compliance, anonymization techniques), consent management, or data sharing agreements** is found in the provided documents.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
